options.content:
## What this dashboard shows

A Go IoT client fleet talks to a server over **one QUIC connection per client**, through an intermediate proxy that injects packet loss, latency, jitter, duplicates, reorders, and bandwidth limits. Each connection carries three simultaneous workloads:

- **Real-time messages (pubsub)** — WebTransport datagrams (unreliable) + streams (reliable)
- **File uploads** — streaming HTTP/3 POST with SHA-256 verification
- **State polling** — plain HTTP/3 GET on a ticker

The proxy is switched between five impairment profiles: `wifi-good`, `cellular-lte`, `cellular-3g`, `satellite`, `flaky` (15% loss). The headline question the dashboard answers: **does one QUIC connection survive every profile without tearing down and reconnecting?** (spoiler — yes; watch `Reconnects` stay at 0).

panels.title: Active profile
panels.description: Name of the impairment profile currently loaded by the proxy, from `quixiot_proxy_profile_info`. A purple annotation line appears on every time-series panel whenever this changes (e.g. via `make restart PROFILE=...`), making before/after comparison obvious.

options.content: Six tiles showing the overall health of the system. If everything else is healthy, **Reconnects** stays at 0 and **Drop rate** tracks whatever the active profile is configured for.

panels.title: Active QUIC connections
panels.description: Number of client devices currently connected to the server over QUIC. Each device keeps one connection open, even though it runs three different workloads (pubsub, uploads, polling) over it. With a 10-client fleet you should see 10.

panels.title: Pubsub subscribers
panels.description: How many subscribers the server is fanning messages out to, summed across all topics. Every `subscriber` or `mixed`-role client adds one per topic it joins.

panels.title: Active proxy sessions
panels.description: Number of client↔server flows the impairment proxy is tracking right now. One session per client. Sessions are evicted after 5 minutes of no traffic.

panels.title: Reconnects (5m rate)
panels.description: Rate at which clients have had to tear down their QUIC connection and redo the handshake. This is the headline QUIC-resilience number: it should stay at 0 even on `flaky` (15% loss). Anything above zero means the connection broke and had to be rebuilt.

panels.title: Drop rate to_server
panels.description: Fraction of client→server packets the proxy is dropping right now (1-minute window). Should fluctuate around the profile's configured `drop_probability` — e.g. ~15% on `flaky`, ~0.2% on `wifi-good`.

panels.title: Throughput
panels.description: Total bytes/sec flowing through the proxy, both directions combined (1-minute window). Dominated by file uploads when the uploader role is active; otherwise dominated by pubsub datagrams and poll responses.

panels.title: What the proxy is doing to the network

options.content: The impairment proxy sits between each client and the server and simulates a flaky network link according to the active profile YAML (`configs/proxy-*.yaml`). These panels show **what the proxy is actually doing** — compare against the profile's configured `drop_probability`, `latency`, `jitter`, and `bandwidth` values.

panels.title: Drop rate by direction
panels.description:
Observed packet-drop rate, split by direction:
- `to_server` = uplink (client → server)
- `to_client` = downlink (server → client)

Compare with the profile's configured `drop_probability`: the live value naturally fluctuates around it.

panels.title: Enforced delay distribution
panels.description: Heatmap of the latency the proxy adds to each packet, **aggregated across both directions** (uplink + downlink). Each profile sets a base `latency` plus a ±`jitter` range — you'll see a band centered on `latency` with width ≈ 2·`jitter`. For a per-direction view, use the `Direction` variable to filter.

panels.title: Packets/sec by direction
panels.description: UDP packets/sec through the proxy, stacked by direction. The shape tells you what the workload is doing: chatty pubsub = high steady rate; file upload = fewer but larger packets (see bytes/sec for that axis).

panels.title: Drops / dupes / reorders rate
panels.description: Per-second count of each impairment the proxy applied, split by direction. Not every profile enables all three — `wifi-good` only drops occasionally; `flaky` applies all of them, which is why it's called flaky.

options.content: The key question for a lossy link: **can one QUIC connection stay up despite continuous packet loss?** QUIC is supposed to tolerate loss transparently — it retransmits lost data on reliable streams and keeps the connection alive via keepalive frames. Reconnects should stay at **zero** through every profile; handshakes should happen only at startup.

panels.title: Client handshake duration
panels.description: How long QUIC+TLS took to establish, per client. Each client does this twice at startup: once for HTTP/3 and once for WebTransport (unless coalesced onto a single connection, which this build does). After the startup burst you should see nothing — any later activity means something reconnected.

panels.title: RTT (smoothed) per client
panels.description: QUIC's internal estimate of round-trip time, per client, from `quic.Connection.ConnectionState()`. On a lossy link this should stay stable — QUIC's congestion control dampens single-packet jitter. If RTT spikes with every drop, something is off.

panels.title: Handshakes vs reconnects (cumulative)
panels.description:
Cumulative counts, not rates:
- **handshakes** (blue) — grows once per client at startup (or twice if H3 and WT aren't coalesced). Should be flat after that.
- **reconnects** (red) — should stay flat at 0 for the entire run. Any growth means a client had to rebuild its connection.

panels.title: Reconnects in selected range
panels.description: Total reconnects across all selected clients within the dashboard's time range. Zero is the healthy value — any non-zero number means a connection broke and had to be rebuilt, i.e. QUIC's loss recovery failed.

panels.title: Median handshake (s)
panels.description: Median time to complete the QUIC+TLS handshake, computed from the histogram. Because QUIC finishes the handshake in 1 RTT (vs 2-3 for TLS-over-TCP), this value is close to the active profile's RTT.

options.content: WebTransport multiplexes many independent streams plus fire-and-forget **datagrams** onto one QUIC connection. Clients publish and subscribe on named topics. Datagrams are fast but unreliable — dropped datagrams are **not** retransmitted (the tradeoff is lower latency). Streams are reliable but subject to head-of-line blocking within each stream.

panels.title: Datagrams in/out by topic
panels.description:
Datagram rate on each WebTransport session, split by direction:
- `out` = this client publishing
- `in` = this client receiving (delivered by the broker)

Per-topic breakdown comes from the `topic` variable.

panels.title: Datagram delivery loss % by topic
panels.description: **One-way** delivery loss, not round-trip. Computed: `(out − in) / out` across all selected clients — how many published datagrams failed to reach subscribers. Treats the fleet as one broadcast domain: if any publisher sends `N` datagrams on a topic, subscribers should collectively receive `N × subscribers`. Expect near-zero on `wifi-good`, visible gaps on `flaky`. This is the headline contrast vs reliable streams, whose loss is always 0 (retransmitted).

targets.expr: 100 * (sum by (topic) (rate(quixiot_client_datagrams_total{direction="out", topic=~"$topic"}[$__rate_interval])) - sum by (topic) (rate(quixiot_client_datagrams_total{direction="in", topic=~"$topic"}[$__rate_interval]))) / clamp_min(sum by (topic) (rate(quixiot_client_datagrams_total{direction="out", topic=~"$topic"}[$__rate_interval])), 1e-9)

panels.title: Publish-to-receive latency p50/p95/p99
panels.description: Wall-clock time from when a publisher stamped a message to when the subscriber received it. Because a `mixed`-role client is both publisher and subscriber on the same process, there's no clock skew. Mixes both datagram and stream surfaces — the stream tail shows retransmissions under loss.

panels.title: Pubsub drops by reason / surface
panels.description:
When the server can't keep up delivering to a subscriber, it drops messages rather than block:
- `surface` — whether the drop was on the datagram path or a stream.
- `reason` — why (queue_full, closed, oversize, …).

Datagram drops are expected under heavy load; stream drops indicate a slow subscriber.

options.content: The other two workloads sharing the same QUIC connection: streaming **file uploads** (`POST /files/*`, body verified with SHA-256) and stateless **polling** (`GET /state` and `GET /config/{id}` on a ticker). The latency tail tells you how each profile impacts bulk transfer vs small request/response.

panels.title: Request rate by path
panels.description:
Server-side HTTP/3 requests per second, broken down by method, path, and HTTP status:
- `/state` — client poller hits it on a ticker
- `/config/{clientID}` — per-client config endpoint
- `/files/{name}` — file upload target
- `/metrics` — Prometheus scraping the server's in-band endpoint

panels.title: Server request latency p50/p95/p99
panels.description: Server-side latency per HTTP/3 handler, split by path. Measured from request arrival to response written. p99 is more sensitive to tail effects from packet loss and bandwidth caps.

panels.title: Upload payload bytes/sec (application-layer)
panels.description: Payload bytes/sec **handed to the server's upload handler**, from `quixiot_server_upload_bytes`. This is application-layer data — smaller than what crosses the wire (QUIC + HTTP/3 framing, plus retransmissions). For raw wire bytes see *Bytes/sec by direction* in the proxy row. Shape: steep ramps during each upload, flat between.

panels.title: Upload duration distribution
panels.description: Heatmap of how long each upload took, from the server's upload handler. Under loss and bandwidth caps the distribution shifts right — `satellite` and `flaky` produce long tails here.

panels.title: Client-measured poll duration p50/p95/p99
panels.description: How long a single poll (`GET /state` or `GET /config/{id}`) took, **measured from the client side** — so it includes the network round-trip, not just server handler time. Compare with *Server request latency* above: the delta is roughly the RTT plus any queuing in the proxy. Under `satellite` (~600 ms RTT) the gap is obvious.

panels.title: Client-measured upload duration p50/p95/p99
panels.description: Total time each upload took, **measured at the client** from request issue to server response received. This includes request upload, server write + SHA-256, and response download. Compare with the server-side *Upload duration distribution*: the gap is network transfer time — most dramatic on `satellite` (high RTT) and `flaky` (loss-driven retransmissions).

options.content: Deeper diagnostics for the impairment proxy — raw byte rates, session lifecycle, and enforced-delay percentiles. Mostly useful when sanity-checking that the proxy is actually doing what the active profile says it should.

panels.title: Bytes/sec by direction
panels.description: Raw UDP bytes/sec through the proxy, per direction. Uploads dominate `to_server`; pubsub delivery and poll responses dominate `to_client`.

panels.title: Sessions over time (active / cumulative)
panels.description:
- **active** (left axis) — proxy NAT entries currently open (gauge). Plateaus at the fleet size.
- **total (cumulative)** (right axis) — every session the proxy has ever tracked in this run (counter). Grows by one per new client; should be flat once the fleet is fully up. If it keeps climbing, NAT entries are cycling (which usually means reconnects).

The two series are on separate Y-axes so the cumulative counter doesn't dwarf the active gauge.

panels.title: Enforced delay p50/p95/p99
panels.description: Percentiles of the latency the proxy adds per packet, split by direction. p50 is near `latency`; p99 is near `latency + jitter`.

options.content:
### Glossary

- **QUIC** — Transport protocol used by HTTP/3. Runs on UDP. Identifies each connection with a Connection ID (not an IP:port tuple), which is why it survives path changes like phone ↔ wifi.
- **HTTP/3** — HTTP semantics running over QUIC. Plain request/response, same as HTTP/2 — different transport.
- **WebTransport** — An API on top of HTTP/3 that exposes many bidirectional streams plus **datagrams** on one QUIC connection. Cheaper than opening separate connections per stream.
- **Stream** — A reliable, in-order byte pipe within a QUIC connection. Loss on a stream is retransmitted. Multiple streams on one connection don't block each other (no head-of-line blocking between streams).
- **Datagram** — An unreliable, unordered, length-delimited message within a QUIC connection. Lost datagrams are **not** retransmitted. Tradeoff: lower latency, no backpressure.
- **RTT (round-trip time)** — Time from sending a packet to getting an ACK back. QUIC completes its handshake in 1 RTT (vs 2-3 for TLS-over-TCP).
- **Handshake** — The initial key-exchange dance. Expensive (TLS + QUIC state). Reused on reconnects.
- **Keepalive** — Small periodic packet sent by the client to prevent idle timeout on the connection. Client-side setting `KeepAlivePeriod` (15s here).
- **Reconnect** — The event a client logs when it has to tear down its QUIC connection and redo the handshake. **In this PoC, reconnects should stay at 0** through every profile.
- **Impairment profile** — YAML file in `configs/` describing the simulated network. Each profile has separate `to_server` and `to_client` settings for `drop_probability`, `latency`, `jitter`, and `bandwidth_bps`.
- **NAT session** — One logical client↔server flow in the proxy, keyed by the client's source `ip:port`. Evicted after 5 minutes idle.
- **Surface** (pubsub) — Which QUIC-level transport carried the message: `datagram` (unreliable) or `stream` (reliable).
- **Direction** — `to_server` = uplink (client → server); `to_client` = downlink (server → client).
